coredata.dc:title: A green approach for synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from waste engine oil: A strategy for waste to value added products
coredata.dc:creator: K, Kalanidhi
V, Bharathi Mohan
P, Nagaraaj
coredata.dc:description: In this present work, waste engine oil was explored as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal route for the first time. The obtained CDs showed excellent water solubility, photostability, bright blue emission under UV light (wavelength 365 nm), pH stability, high quantum yield, and greater biocompatibility. It was thoroughly studied by vital characterization techniques such as HR-TEM, UV–Visible spectroscopy, FTIR, SEM, PSA, Fluorescence spectroscopy with lifetime analysis. The fluorescence of synthesized CDs was quenched by the addition of Fe3+ as it showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+. The sensing probe showed a good linear response (R2 = 0.9986) with a limit of detection 0.055 μM in the linear range of 0.6–3.3 μM. Due to excellent fluorescence properties of CDs, it have been applied as fluorescent ink for information storage. In addition, CDs coated polymer matrix was made and used for bead-based detection of Fe3+. Finally, the presented system could be considered as an efficient probe for Fe3+ detection and paved a way for efficient utilization of waste into value-added products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Waste engine oil
Fe3+ detection
Fluorescent ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size and surface morphology of synthesized CDs
Effect of pH, storage time, and NaCl concentration
Formation mechanism of CDs from engine oil

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author thanks to University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India for providing NFOBC fellowship (NFO-2018-19-OBC-TAM-79393) and the authors also thank to Department of Chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM analysis (a-c) (different magnifications 2 nm, 5 nm, and 20 nm) (d) size distribution (e) PSA analysis, (f) SEM images of carbon dots at 10 μm (g) SEM images of carbon dots at 50 μm, (h) Elemental mapping (Red = carbon, Green = Nitrogen, Blue = Oxygen).
Absorbance (a) and emission (b) spectra of CDs (inset: blue emission under UV lamp).
Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dots at increasing wavelength of 20 nm from 300 to 440 nm.
(a) Effect of pH, (b) storage time and (c) NaCl concentration on fluorescence intensity of CDs.
Fluorescence quenching rate of CDs with different metal ions.
Photographic images of CDs (a) uncoated, (b) coated in the absence of Fe3+ , (c) coated in the presence of Fe3+ on polymer matrix under UV lamp.
(a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentration of Fe3+
(b) Relation between fluorescence intensity of CDs and concentration Fe3+
(c) Linear response curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration Fe3+.
Photographic images of CDs written on TLC plate under (a) visible light and (b) UV lamp (wavelength 365 nm).
Synthesis of CDs from waste engine oil
Schematic representation of interaction of CDs and Fe3+
Fluorescence lifetime measurement of CDs with and without Fe3+.
Comparison of waste derived CDs for Fe3+ sensing with previous literatures.

row.entry: N, P doped carbon quantum dots

head.ce:title: A green approach for synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from waste engine oil: A strategy for waste to value added products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai 600025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai-600025

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this present work, waste engine oil was explored as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal route for the first time. The obtained CDs showed excellent water solubility, photostability, bright blue emission under UV light (wavelength 365 nm), pH stability, high quantum yield, and greater biocompatibility. It was thoroughly studied by vital characterization techniques such as HR-TEM, UV–Visible spectroscopy, FTIR, SEM, PSA, Fluorescence spectroscopy with lifetime analysis. The fluorescence of synthesized CDs was quenched by the addition of Fe3+ as it showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+. The sensing probe showed a good linear response (R2 = 0.9986) with a limit of detection 0.055 μM in the linear range of 0.6–3.3 μM. Due to excellent fluorescence properties of CDs, it have been applied as fluorescent ink for information storage. In addition, CDs coated polymer matrix was made and used for bead-based detection of Fe3+. Finally, the presented system could be considered as an efficient probe for Fe3+ detection and paved a way for efficient utilization of waste into value-added products.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ions play a crucial role in human body as an essential element and are involved in various biological processes such as hemoglobin synthesis, enzyme catalysis, and oxygen transportation. At the same time, overdose or excess of Fe3+ ion can lead to severe health issues such as pakinson's disease and cytotoxicity [1–4]. Furthermore, the higher concentration of Fe3+ can cause serious environmental problems. For these reasons, demonstrating fast, accurate, feasible, sensitive and high-performance analytical techniques has gained increased attention [5]. Recently, various analytical techniques have been performed for Fe3+ detection such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and electrochemical method. However, it needs tedious sample analysis procedure, modern instrumentation, and issues in terms of reproducibility and reliability, which reduce their applications [6]. Recently, fluorescence based detection is gaining more importance due to its simplicity, sensitivity and accuracy. CDs are one of environmental benign fluorescence carbon materials, holding remarkable properties such as biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence properties, ease surface functionalization, facile fabrication, chemical inertness, low cost, high storage stability, and water solubility [7–11]. Moreover, it has been widely explored in various fields such as sensors [12–16], bioimaging [17–20], supercapacitor [21], and photocatalysis [22,23], antimicrobial activity [24] etc.

Different precursors have been employed for the preparation of CDs. However, effective utilization of waste materials is gaining paramount attention towards researchers in the area of waste management and it is a major step taken towards minimizing environmental pollution [25,26]. Many attempts have been made for the preparation of CDs from waste materials as a precursor. Wang et al. have reported the conversion of inexpensive biomass waste (orange peel) into carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal route with a quantum yield of 4.26% [27]. Hu et al. have proposed a facile approach for the reuse of waste frying oil as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots with a quantum yield of 3.66% [28]. Similarly, Hu et al. have developed an environmentally benign route of reusing the polyethylene terephthalate (PET) for producing carbon dots with the assistance of H2O2 with a quantum yield of 5.2% [29]. Surendran et al. have developed a facile approach for utilizing agro-waste Ananas comosus for the preparation of carbon quantum dots by simple hydrothermal route with a quantum yield of 10.65% [30]. Gunjal et al. have demonstrated a simple and cost effective route for the synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots from tea waste residue by chemical oxidation method with a quantum yield 2.47% [18]. Su et al. have developed the biomass waste derived carbon dots in subcritical water with a quantum yield of 8.64% [31]. Park et al. have proposed a green route for large-scale synthesis of carbon nanodots from food waste derived sources with an internal quantum yield of 2.85% [32]. However, the quantum yield obtained from the above waste derived CDs was comparatively low. Moreover, much of the literature were focused on biomass waste derived CDs for different applications. Since it is biodegradable, it could not cause the toxic effects as it is disposed to the environment. A few research works have given importance to the environmental polluting waste into value added products [33,34]. Considering this in mind, proposing a green route for producing CDs from non-degradable waste materials with comparable quantum yield is gaining much attention.

Vehicle plays a vital role in our day-to-day and also the number of vehicles/ machines gets increasing constantly. They separately use engine oil (motor oil) as a lubricant for engine parts to avoid friction and prevent the damage of metallic engine parts. Thereby it produces tons of waste engine oil. The disposal of this waste oil is a challenging task. Interestingly, we have used waste engine oil as a precursor for formation of CDs for the first time. Moreover, the disposal of waste / reused engine can cause serious environmental problems such as water pollution as it is harmful to aquatic species and affect the soil quality. Considering the potentially toxic effects of waste engine oil on the environment, a new initiative has been taken to convert this pollutant into value-added products like carbon dots since it contains a vast amount of carbon content. The composition of engine oil contains a maximum of the mixture of low and high molecular weight aliphatic, aromatic hydrocarbons and other elements such as nitrogen and sulfur as it offers self-doping in the synthesis process. Therefore, it could be a qualified carbon precursor for the preparation of CDs. In this connection, exploring a novel, green and an environmentally benign route for managing waste and in turn, producing CDs as an efficient sensing nanoprobe with high quantum yield and utilizes the strategy called waste to a valuable product.

In this present work, we have developed a facile and green way of synthesizing CDs from waste engine oil as a precursor for the first time by a simple and greener hydrothermal route with the support of H2O2. Hydrogen peroxide is used as an environmentally benign oxidant for the preparation of CDs. Overall; this work offers the advantages of 1) usage of no hazard chemicals (2) one-step synthesis process (3) green reagents used (4) waste minimization. It gives an excellent way for the managing waste and reuses the waste engine oil as a precursor material which offers simplicity, economical feasibility and eco-friendliness with a quantum yield of 11.4%. It shows excellent fluorescence quenching response towards Fe3+, demonstrating that the synthesized sensing probe has good selectivity and sensitivity over Fe3+ with a detection limit as low as 0.055 μM which implies that this material is an excellent candidate for environmental detection of Fe3+. Further, it can be explored as an environmentally benign fluorescent ink for information storage and anti-counterfeiting applications. In addition, bead-based detection of Fe3+ is also demonstrated using CDs coated polymer matrix.

Engine oil (used) was collected from the vehicle. All metal salts (FeCl3, ZnSO4, CdCl2, CuSO4, FeCl2, NaSO4, NaCl, CuCl2, CuCl3, Pb(NO3)2, HgCl2, KCl, MnSO4, Co(NO3)2, AgNO3 and Quinine sulfate were purchased from sigma-aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (30 wt%), polymer matrix (XAD-7) was procured from Merck. All purchased reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. The distilled water was used throughout the work to carry out the experiments.

Waste/used engine oil was used as a precursor for the preparation of CDs. It can be reused by a simple filtration process. It is filtered primarily through normal filter paper and again filtered through whatmann filter paper 41 (pore size: 20–25 μm) to remove combustion products like coke and some metal impurity due to the friction of engine parts.

CDs were synthesized by simple hydrothermal employing waste engine oil as a precursor for the first time without adding any surface passivating agent (Scheme 1 ). The optimization of reaction conditions was carried out for the synthesis of CDs from engine oil with the support of hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%). Initially, the oil to hydrogen peroxide ratio of 1:5 was taken and reaction was preceded with the temperature of 180 °C for 12 h. The resultant product shows the incomplete formation of CDs. With the same condition, only the temperature of the reaction was increased to 200 and 220 °C which also exhibits the incomplete formation of CDs. Later, the ratio of oil to peroxide was increased to 1:10 with the reaction temperature of 180 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C for 12 h. The complete conversion of waste oil to CDs was achieved at 220 °C whereas reaction temperature 180 °C, 200 °C exhibits the formation of a turbid solution. Overall results claim that the optimum conditions for the formation of CDs from engine oil are oil to peroxide ratio of 1:10, processing temperature of 220 °C for 12 h. In brief, 1 mL of purified engine oil was mixed with 10 mL of H2O2 (1:10 ratio) then transferred to 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. After that, it was naturally cooled to room temperature. The yellow colored aqueous solution was obtained as it indicates the formation of CDs. It was washed with dichloromethane to remove the organic impurities. Further, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose nitrate membrane to remove the non-fluorescent particles and stored at room temperature for future use.

The quantum yield of CDs was calculated by reported procedure. Quinine sulphate solution (0.1 M H2SO4, QY: 0.54) was used as a reference. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs at excitation wavelength of 320 nm was calculated by following formula,
Q =
Q R
∗
I S
/
I R
∗
A R
/
A S
∗
ɳ 2
S
/
ɳ 2
R
where Q represents quantum yield, I is fluorescent intensity, A refers absorbance measured at excitation wavelength, ɳ is refractive index of solvent. The subscripts R and S represent reference and sample respectively.

10 μL (1 mg/mL) of CDs solution was added into 3 mL of distilled water in which different concentrations of Fe3+ were added. After that, it was allowed to stand for 5 mins for the interaction between CDs and Fe3+. Then, the fluorescence measurement was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Similarly, different metal ions (300 μM) were added to CDs solution and recorded to perform the selectivity of the present sensing system. All measurements were performed in triplicate at room temperature.

The prepared CDs can be further explored as fluorescent ink due to their excellent emission properties. For this, the obtained fluorescent CDs solution was loaded into the capillary tube, and the pattern was written on the TLC (Thin layer chromatography) plate (showed no fluorescence in UV light) using CDs filled capillary tube. After that, it was dried and exposed under UV light to visualize the written pattern on the TLC plate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and surface morphology of synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HR-TEM (High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscope) analysis helps to examine the surface morphology and size of the particles. Fig. 1 (a–d) displays the HR-TEM image of the obtained CDs with different magnifications and size distribution. It exhibits homogenous, well distributed spherical particles in the range of 2–10 nm with an average size of 5.9 nm and interlayer spacing value of 0.2142 nm.

The size of synthesized CDs was also evidenced from particle size analyser and it is also acknowledged the same size range with PDI value of 0.443 [35] (Fig. 1(e)). The diffused rings were displayed in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern which indicates the amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs (inset Fig. 1(b)).

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis is studied to characterize the surface morphology of the CDs. The SEM image of the CDs was displayed in Fig. 1(f, g). The synthesized CDs exhibit spherical morphology and uniformly distributed with partial agglomeration. The elemental analysis is shown in Fig. 1(h) which reveals the existence of carbon (44 wt%), nitrogen (4 wt%), and oxygen (52 wt%) in the formed CDs. These results confirm the presence of large amount of oxygen and nitrogen containing active functional sites over the surface of the CDs as displayed in the elemental mapping (Red = carbon, Green = Nitrogen, Blue = Oxygen) [2,25].

UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopies are used to study the optical properties of the synthesized CDs. The UV–visible spectrum of waste engine oil (Fig. S1) and CDs were displayed in the Fig. 2(a). UV–Visible spectrum of waste engine oil exhibits an absorbance peak at 293 nm whereas CDs showed an optical absorption peak centered at 262 nm which is corresponding to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon structure. Fig. 2(b) displays the equivalent photoluminescence spectrum of CDs for the absorbance. Under visible light, it appears pale yellow whereas when it is viewed under UV lamp (365 nm), it exhibits a bright blue emission feature (inset Fig. 2). The photoluminescence spectrum of CDs was obtained by exciting the material with different excitation wavelengths starting from 300 nm with an increment of 20 nm in order to evaluate the maximum emission intensity at a particular excitation wavelength. The PL spectrum showed maximum fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 3 ). By increasing the excitation wavelength, the emission intensity decreases gradually along with shifting of the spectrum towards longer wavelength (red shift). This excitation-dependent emission property of synthesized CDs was due to the existence of different sizes of the particles, different surface defects, and states. Moreover, the origin of the fluorescence of CDs still needs discussion. In general, CDs have aromatic carbon core structure with plethora amount oxygen containing surface functionalities which might be the evidence for the origin of fluorescence. The most commonly reported mechanism for fluorescence of CDs is oxygen functionality and surface defects due to surface oxidation, quantum confinement effect, different surface state, aromatic structure and recombination of electron-hole [36–44]. In addition, the fluorescent property of CDs was studied by calculating the fluorescence quantum yield. The quantum yield of the synthesized CDs was calculated to be 11.4% where quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 0.54) was used as a reference (Fig. S2).

The FTIR spectrum helps to analyze the functional groups present on the synthesized CDs. Fig. 4 shows the broad peak around 3318 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching vibration. A peak at 1636 cm−1 is ascribed to C=O/C=C stretching vibration. A weak absorption peak at 1276 cm−1 is assigned to CO stretching vibration. All the presented absorption peaks represent the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functionality in the synthesized CDs [45,46]. The obtained results confirm the existence of active functional groups on the surface which helps in increasing the hydrophilic nature of CDs for higher water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH, storage time, and NaCl concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs were further evaluated with different parameters such as pH, ionic strength, and storage time. Firstly, the influence of pH was tested against emission intensity of CDs by adding the different pH solutions ranging from 2 to 12 as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The fluorescence emission of CDs remains constant at pH 6–8 whereas it shows a modest reduction in emission intensity at lower (pH < 4) and higher (pH > 10) pH values which might be due to the aggregation of CDs caused by non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding is formed between hydroxyl, carboxyl functional groups of CDs at higher pH values [33]. Therefore, pH 7 has been taken for further analysis. Secondly, the effect of storage time against emission intensity was studied and displayed in Fig. 5(b). It shows no change in the emission intensity with storage time as it remains stable for 60 days which indicates the high storage stability of CDs [47]. Finally, it shows excellent stability towards an ionic concentration as it displays stable fluorescence emission over the ionic concentration range from 0.2-1 M [48]. Overall results confirm that as synthesized CDs were stable over different influencing factors such as pH, storage time and ionic concentrations and hence it is suitable for wide range of applications.

The selectivity of the sensing probe was performed by subjecting CDs to different metal cations such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag2+, K+ , Hg2+, Co2+ and Mn2+ , and corresponding fluorescence measurement was recorded. Among all, Fe3+ exhibits a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs whereas other metal ions show little or negligible effects (Fig. 6 ). This phenomenon of fluorescence quenching clearly states that the present sensing system is highly selective towards Fe3+ over other metal cations due to the strong affinity existing between Fe3+ and CDs. The bead based Fe3+ detection was demonstrated using polymer matrix (XAD-7). Fig. 7(a-c) illustrates the uncoated and CDs coated polymer matrix in the absence and presence of Fe3+ under UV lamp. The uncoated polymer matrix has no fluorescence whereas CDs coated polymer matrix exhibits bright fluorescence and it gets quenched in the presence of Fe3+ under UV lamp. It clearly claims that polymer bead based detection also confirms the sensitivity of the system towards Fe3+.

Later, a competition experiment was studied for CDs towards Fe3+ sensing (Fig.S3). The CDs containing different metal ions solution were made in which Fe3+ solution was added; it exhibits comparable and similar fluorescence quenching effect as that of the quenching effect with only the presence of Fe3+. It clearly explains that the synthesized CDs exhibit excellent selectivity over the existence of other competitive metal ions.

To analyze the sensitivity of the sensing system, different concentrations of the iron solution were added into CDs and corresponding absorbance and emission spectrum were recorded. The absorbance spectrum of CDs with and without Fe3+ was evaluated and displayed in Fig. S4. It showed an increase in absorbance value with increasing concentration of Fe3+ which also accompanied with the broadening of peak around 265 nm. This phenomenon clearly explains the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ ions. Fig. 8(a) displays the emission spectrum of CDs. It was decreased gradually with increasing concentration of Fe3+ which is due to strong affinity between CDs and Fe3+. A correlation between emission intensity and concentration of Fe3+ is displayed in Fig. 8(b). A good linear response (R2 = 0.9986) has been obtained between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.055 μM in the range of 0.6–3.3 μM (Fig. 8(c)). These results claim that the presented sensor is highly sensitive towards Fe3+. According to U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the permissible limit of Fe3+ is 5.4 μM in drinking water [42,47]. The obtained LOD value was satisfactory and detect as low as 0.055 μM in the environmental samples. This LOD value is better and lower than the previous reports as illustrated in Table 2. (See Fig. 8.)

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was explained by the presence of surface functional groups such as OH and COOH, which form a strong complex with Fe3+ (Scheme 2 .). When Fe3+ solution was introduced into CDs, it has undergone non-radiative electron transfer process by forming a complex with Fe3+ through the surface active groups. During this interaction, there is the transfer of electron from the excited state of CDs to unfilled Fe3+ d-orbital which is accompanied with non- radiative electron/hole recombination as results of fluorescence of CDs gets quenched [49,50]. The quenching mechanism of CDs is explained by the fluorescence lifetime analysis as mentioned in Table 1 . The fluorescent lifetime of CDs (Fig. S5) is 4.98 ns whereas fluorescence lifetime of CDs with the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S6) is found to be 4.96 ns. There is no significant change in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ . These results clearly indicate that the fluorescence mechanism of CDs is static quenching with the formation of ground state complex between CDs and Fe3+ [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of CDs from engine oil
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed formation mechanism of CDs from engine oil is explained by the pivotal role of H2O2 in the carbonization reaction. It has undergone a decomposition process by producing OH
radicals under high temperature and pressure. These radicals can initiate the formation of CDs by cutting the long-chain aromatic, aliphatic carbon into small chains which are presented in the engine oil. These small chains are degraded into fragments which are further decomposed into smaller molecules. By the process of condensation and polymerization, these smaller molecules combined with each other and form a soluble polymer. Subsequently, these polymers have undergone further polymerization due to intermolecular dehydration process leading to the formation of CDs [51,52].

The CDs have been considered as an inspired candidate due to their impressive properties. Though it has been explored as a sensitive nanoprobe for metal ion detection, it can be considered as a cost effective fluorescent ink for information storage and anti-counterfeiting application due to its excellent emission properties. The photographic image of CDs as fluorescent ink is displayed in Fig. 9 . The letters written on the TLC plate are unseen under normal light (Fig. 9(a)) and shine intensely when exposed to UV light (Fig. 9(b)) that helping to transfer confidential information. The written letters on TLC plate exhibit remarkable stability over several months. Hence, it could be a potential replacement for traditional fluorescent ink in terms of cost effectiveness, stability, eco-friendliness [53].

In summary, excellent fluorescent CDs have been successfully prepared by facile hydrothermal treatment of waste engine oil with the support of hydrogen peroxide for the first time. As synthesized CDs possess water solubility, excellent storage stability, high quantum yield, pH stability, biocompatibity, and exhibit excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ over other metal ions investigated with a limit of detection of 0.05 μM. Apart from this, it could be considered as nontoxic, cost effective fluorescence ink due to its remarkable fluorescence properties. This work explores waste engine oil as a value added precursor for the formation of highly fluorescent CDs for Fe3+ detection. In addition, bead-based Fe3+ detection is also proposed using CDs coated polymer matrix. Finally, the proposed strategy is green, novel, and encourages utilizing the waste materials into value-added products.

Kalanidhi K - Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Bharathi Mohan V - Resources, Methodology. Dr. Nagaraaj P - Technical correction, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors whose names K. Kalanidhi, V. Bharathi Mohan P. Nagaraaj certify that their affiliation is Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai-25. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thanks to University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India for providing NFOBC fellowship (NFO-2018-19-OBC-TAM-79393) and the authors also thank to Department of Chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108724.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from vitamin B1 for highly selective detection of Fe3+ ion
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 1. F. Wu, M. Yang, H. Zhang, S. Zhu, X. Zhu, and K. Wang. Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots from vitamin B1 for highly selective detection of Fe3+ ion. Optical Materials 77 (2018) 258-263.

J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

Mater. Lett.

Arab. J. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples
